frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Arre, shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor aur mangalsutra pehne toh ladki suhagan jaisi lagti hai, poori si.
B: Aajkal office wali ladkiyan nahi pehenti na.
A: Haan choice toh unki hai, par hamari parampara bhi koi cheez hoti hai, aur pati ke liye shagun maana jata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as making a woman 'complete' and ties them to tradition and the husband's wellbeing. It encodes pressure on married women to display sindoor/mangalsutra while masking control as culture and personal choice.
original_frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 52. Married. A woman in Jaipur. Your son Rohit who is 28 just started seeing a girl named Divya. Rohit brought Divya home for the first time last Sunday. Divya was wearing full makeup. You are on a phone call with your sister telling her about meeting Divya for the first time. You are describing Divya's appearance in detail and what you think the heavy makeup says about her and whether she is the right kind of girl for your son and what she might be like without it.
